retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A voluntary Glanzmann thrombasthenia registry enrolled patients with GT, and their data indicates that the first symptoms occurred at a median age of 1one year and a mean of 5.6 years. Of the enrollees, 85% were diagnosed with GT by 14 years of age. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Descibe the diagnostic options for evaluation of suspected Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Explain the treatment options for Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Identify the importance of an interprofessional team to optimize outcomes for patients with Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of GT is estimated to be about 1:1,000,000 in the general population. [2] In certain areas, such as those with high consanguinity, the prevalence is roughly 1:200,000 or higher. [3] [6] Pakistan and the Canadian provinces Newfoundland and Labrador are some of the areas with a high prevalence. [9] [6] Some patients may have mild symptoms and are never identified as having GT, so the actual prevalence may be higher than reported. [6] Studies show that females are slightly more frequently affected than males. GT is commonly encountered in children and young adults but can affect any age. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type I -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Key factors to bear in mind in the management of GSD I include: Early intervention with dietary management is crucial for preventing irreversible cognitive and physical damage. The use of G-CSF in GSD Ib is critical for reducing infection risk due to neutropenia. Monitoring for renal and hepatic complications should be a lifelong process.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type II -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for IOPD includes: Spinal muscular atrophy 1: Differs from IOPD because no cardiac muscle is involved Danon disease: Differs from IOPD because inheritance is X-linked [54] The differential diagnosis for LOPD includes: Limb-girdle muscular dystrophy: Differs from LOPD because axial muscles are not affected Duchenne-Becker muscular dystrophy: Differs from LOPD because inheritance is X-linked Other glycogen storage disorders: May be considered; mainly differentiated by a lack of hypoglycemia in glycogen storage disease type II (see Image . Glycogen Storage Disease Types)

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: GT is an autosomal recessive disorder with mutations involving chromosome 17q21, specifically the ITGA2B or ITGB3 genes. [2] [4] [5] Mutations of either gene can cause GT, and hundreds of mutations have been reported. GT results when a patient is homozygous for the same mutation or is a compound heterozygote for different mutations. [3] The various genetic alterations can lead to different levels of function and expression of the alpha IIb beta3 integrin. [2] The Manouche mutation has no alpha IIb beta3 integrin expression. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Gaucher Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Despite knowing the precise genetic causes of most forms of Gaucher disease, the exact cause remains unknown. Patients with the same mutation can have very different signs and symptoms. It is also possible for patients with similar signs and symptoms to have very different genetic mutations. Environmental factors, as well as an individual's particular genetic makeup, most likely influence the phenotypic expression of Gaucher disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type II -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation of glycogen storage disease type II varies widely and is influenced by the age at which symptoms appear. The degree of clinical severity, the extent of tissue damage, and the timing of onset are closely related to the specific mutations involved and the remaining GAA enzymatic activity. [3] The glycogen storage disease type II phenotypes that have been described are IOPD, the classic and more severe type, and LOPD, which can manifest in both childhood and adulthood.

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: GT is a severe bleeding disorder, and life-threatening or fatal bleeding can occur spontaneously or with invasive procedures, severe trauma, or as a consequence of childbirth. [3] [6] The broad mutational causes of GT yield many clinical presentations that range in severity. Patients can do well with healthcare team preparation and coordination, such as the 52-year-old man with GT who had a successful open aortic valve replacement. [10] In general, GT bleeding episodes are not as life-threatening as those associated with inherited coagulation disorders. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Scheuermann Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There is a hereditary component associated with this condition, although the exact mode of transmission is still unclear. This is supported by the fact that incidence is higher in monozygotic versus dizygotic twins [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a congenital bleeding disorder caused by a deficiency of the platelet integrin alpha IIb beta3. This integrin is the platelet fibrinogen receptor and is thus essential to platelet aggregation and hemostasis. Patients with Glanzmann thrombasthenia have lifelong bleeding episodes that often involve the mucocutaneous membranes. This activity reviews the clinical presentation, evaluation, and treatment options for Glanzmann thrombasthenia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Gaucher Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Gaucher disease (pronounced as GO-SHEY) is an autosomal recessive inborn error of metabolism caused by mutations in the glucocerebrosidase ( GBA1 ) gene [1] . GBA1 is an enzyme that cleaves the beta-glucosidic linkage of glucocerebroside lipids.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The true incidence of metabolic diseases is difficult to determine, given the lack of uniform, universal screening at birth. While GSD type IX has been found to be the most common subtype, the individual incidence of specific GSD types is further complicated due to overlapping symptoms and the lack of standardized specific testing in most areas of the world.

retrieved_docs.content: B) Niemann-Pick diseases A, B, and C (transmembrane protein defect C1, soluble non-enzymatic protein defect C2) C) Gaucher disease types 1, 2, and 3 D) Fabry disease (classic and late-onset types) E) Metachromatic leukodystrophy F) Globoid leukodystrophy (Krabbe disease) G) GM1 gangliosidosis types 1, 2, and 3 H) Multiple sulfatase deficiency II. Oligosaccharidosis (glycoproteins; all disorders with mutant enzymes): Alfa mannosidosis Schindler disease Aspartylglucosaminuria Fucosidosis III. Mucopolysaccharidosis (MPS) (mucopolysaccharides a.k.a glycosaminoglycans (GAG); all disorders with mutant enzymes): Hurler syndrome Scheie syndrome Hurler-Scheie syndrome Hunter syndrome SanFilippo syndrome A, B, C, and D Morquio syndrome A and B Maroteaux-Lamy syndrome Sly syndrome IV. retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type II -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Newborn Screening
retrieved_docs.content: According to Prosser et al, implementing NBS for 4 million infants annually in the United States could detect approximately 134 cases of glycogen storage disease type II, including 40 cases of IOPD, compared to only 36 cases identified through clinical presentation alone. NBS would also uncover 94 cases of LOPD, many of which might remain asymptomatic for years or even decades. Screening for IOPD specifically could help prevent 13 deaths and identify 26 children at risk of requiring mechanical ventilation by the age of 36 months. [76] To confirm the diagnoses, GAA is crucial, particularly for IOPD cases where specific mutations are associated with severe clinical manifestations. [77]

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease Type I -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for GSD I patients has dramatically improved with enhanced metabolic management and early diagnosis. With optimal treatment, many patients reach adulthood and lead relatively normal lives. However, they continue to face risks of liver complications, including hepatocellular adenomas and HCC, which necessitate regular imaging, such as ultrasound or MRI, for early detection [3] , as well as renal complications and cardiovascular issues later in life. Liver transplantation may be considered for patients with severe liver involvement or complications that do not respond to conventional management. [12]

This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1731
retrieved_docs.content: Kidney glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى) is a primary renal pathology that can give rise to uremia (اليوريمية) as a downstream clinical manifestation. In addition, the reproductive disorder (اضطراب بولي تناسلي) is classified as a condition that falls under the broader category of kidney glomerular disease, indicating a relationship between glomerular renal pathology and reproductive system involvement. Recognizing that kidney glomerular disease may present with systemic effects such as uremia and may be associated with reproductive abnormalities helps clinicians provide comprehensive assessment and management for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: for hereditary‑degenerative conditions, many of which are encompassed by the SMQ category that bridges congenital, genitourinary, infectious, and neuro‑degenerative disease groups. Recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and safe medication selection in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

This integrated network highlights how diverse vascular conditions—ranging from systemic circulatory disorders to localized retinal vascular lesions—are interrelated, informing a comprehensive approach to diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2619

ULTRASOUND PROCEDURES ON THE URINARY AND MALE GENITAL SYSTEMS includes the child procedure فحص الكلى بالموجات فوق الصوتية, and فحص الكلى بالموجات فوق الصوتية is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية, underscoring the role of renal ultrasound as a diagnostic tool in evaluating patients with end‑stage renal disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how systemic disorders, renal pathology, and imaging modalities are interconnected in the assessment and management of severe kidney disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2621
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerulopathy) is a disorder affecting the glomeruli of the kidneys, and it can progress to اليوريمية (uremia), a condition characterized by the accumulation of nitrogenous waste products in the blood due to impaired renal filtration. This connection highlights that damage to the kidney’s filtering units may lead to systemic toxicity, emphasizing the need for early identification and treatment of glomerular disease to prevent the development of uremic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: congenital anomalies** contain hereditary and genetic disorders, which also comprise **other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions** and sexually transmitted infections; this hereditary‑genetic group is a member of both **فالج طفلي** and **متلازمة ستيكلر**. Collectively, the integrated structure demonstrates how endocrine, metabolic, fluid‑electrolyte, renal, infectious, vascular, otolaryngologic, behavioral, psychiatric, genetic, and developmental concepts intersect, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment, risk stratification, and multidisciplinary clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Juvenile nephropathic cystinosis (disorder) is a genetic kidney disease that can progress to end‑stage renal disease, described in the triples as “مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية.” In addition, the ocular complication “اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ” (diabetic retinopathy) is clinically associated with “مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية,” indicating that severe kidney failure often co‑occurs with this retinal microvascular disease. Together, these relationships highlight that both juvenile nephropathic cystinosis and diabetic retinopathy share a common endpoint of end‑stage renal disease, underscoring the importance of monitoring renal function in patients with these conditions and integrating kidney and eye care to improve overall patient outcomes.

Additionally, the disease referred to in Arabic as “داء ريفسام” manifests its clinical findings in the retina, reinforcing that retinal involvement—whether from systemic metabolic disorders, primary retinal diseases, or targeted retinal lesion destruction—requires precise identification of the retinal site for accurate diagnosis and treatment.
